mn21:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn21:10.1: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, here some *bhikkhu* is gentle only so long as disagreeable kinds of speech do not touch him; mild only so long as disagreeable kinds of speech do not touch him; calm only so long as disagreeable kinds of speech do not touch him.
mn21:10.2: But when disagreeable kinds of speech touch a *bhikkhu*, then he should be known as gentle, he should be known as mild, he should be known as calm.
mn21:10.3: I do not call that *bhikkhu* ‘easy to advise’ who is easy to advise and acquires amenability merely for the sake of robe, almsfood, lodging, and medicines and supplies for the sick.
mn21:10.5: Because, *bhikkhus*, when that *bhikkhu* does not receive robe, almsfood, lodging, and medicines and supplies for the sick, he is not easy to advise and does not acquire amenability.
mn21:10.6: But the *bhikkhu* who is easy to advise and acquires amenability because he honors *dhamma* alone, regards *dhamma* alone as important, respects *dhamma* alone, esteems *dhamma* alone, and reveres *dhamma* alone—that one I call ‘easy to advise.’
mn21:10.7: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, you should train thus: ‘Honoring *dhamma* alone, regarding *dhamma* alone as important, respecting *dhamma* alone, esteeming *dhamma* alone, and revering *dhamma* alone, we will be easy to advise and will acquire amenability.’
mn21:10.8: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:11.1: These are the five ways of speaking, *bhikkhus*, by which others, when speaking to you, might address you—
mn21:11.10: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you beneficially or unbeneficially;
mn21:11.11: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*] or with hate inside [*dosantarā*].
mn21:11.12: Even there, *bhikkhus*, you should train thus:
mn21:11.13: ‘Our mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and we will not utter evil speech. We will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*], with no hate inside [*na dosantarā*].
mn21:11.14: And we will dwell pervading that person with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettāsahagatena cetasā*], and with that as the basis we will dwell pervading the whole world with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettāsahagatena cittena*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.’
mn21:11.15: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:11.6: with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*] or with hate inside [*dosantarā*].
mn21:11.7: When others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you in a timely way or in an untimely way;
mn21:11.8: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you truthfully or falsely;
mn21:11.9: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you gently or harshly;
mn21:12.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were to come carrying a hoe and a basket.
mn21:12.10: "Because, bhante, this great earth is deep and immeasurable.
mn21:12.11: It is not easy to make it into no earth;
mn21:12.12: that man would only reap weariness and frustration."
mn21:12.3: ‘I will make this great earth into no earth.’
mn21:12.4: There and there he would dig it up, there and there he would scatter it, there and there he would spit on it, there and there he would urinate on it:
mn21:12.7: could that man make this great earth into no earth?"
mn21:13.1: "In the same way, *bhikkhus*, these are the five ways of speaking by which others, when speaking to you, might address you—
mn21:13.10: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you beneficially or unbeneficially;
mn21:13.11: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*] or with hate inside [*dosantarā*].
mn21:13.12: Even there, *bhikkhus*, you should train thus:
mn21:13.13: ‘Our mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and we will not utter evil speech. We will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*], with no hate inside [*na dosantarā*].
mn21:13.14: And we will dwell pervading that person with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettāsahagatena cetasā*], and with that as the basis we will dwell pervading the whole world with a mind like the earth [*pathavisamena cetasā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.’
mn21:13.15: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:13.6: with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*] or with hate inside [*dosantarā*].
mn21:13.7: When others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you in a timely way or in an untimely way;
mn21:13.8: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you truthfully or falsely;
mn21:13.9: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you gently or harshly;
mn21:14.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were to come carrying lac, turmeric, blue dye, or madder.
mn21:14.10: that man would only reap weariness and frustration."
mn21:14.3: ‘I will draw a form in this sky; I will make a manifestation of form appear.’
mn21:14.5: could that man draw a form in this sky and make a manifestation of form appear?"
mn21:14.8: "Because, bhante, the sky is formless and invisible.
mn21:14.9: It is not easy there to draw a form or make a manifestation of form appear;
mn21:15.1: "In the same way, *bhikkhus*, these are the five ways of speaking by which others, when speaking to you, might address you—timely or untimely …
mn21:15.2: and with that as the basis we will dwell pervading the whole world with a mind like space [*ākāsasamena cetasā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.’
mn21:15.3: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:16.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were to come carrying a burning grass torch.
mn21:16.10: that man would only reap weariness and frustration."
mn21:16.3: ‘With this burning grass torch I will heat up the river Ganges and make it boil.’
mn21:16.5: could that man, with a burning grass torch, heat up the river Ganges and make it boil?"
mn21:16.8: "Because, bhante, the river Ganges is deep and immeasurable.
mn21:16.9: It is not easy to heat it up and make it boil with a burning grass torch;
mn21:17.1: "In the same way, *bhikkhus*, these are the five ways of speaking by which others, when speaking to you, might address you—timely or untimely …
mn21:17.2: and with that as the basis we will dwell pervading the whole world with a mind like the Ganges [*gaṅgāsamena cetasā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.’
mn21:17.3: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:18.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a cat-skin bag that had been worked, well worked, thoroughly worked, soft, cottony, with its rustling cut off, with its crackling cut off.
mn21:18.10: It is not easy to make it rustle and crackle with a stick or a potsherd;
mn21:18.11: that man would only reap weariness and frustration."
mn21:18.2: Then a man might come carrying a stick or a potsherd.
mn21:18.4: ‘With this stick or potsherd I will make this cat-skin bag, worked, well worked, thoroughly worked, soft, cottony, with its rustling cut off, with its crackling cut off, rustle and crackle.’
mn21:18.6: could that man, with a stick or a potsherd, make that cat-skin bag, worked, well worked, thoroughly worked, soft, cottony, with its rustling cut off, with its crackling cut off, rustle and crackle?"
mn21:18.9: "Because, bhante, that cat-skin bag has been worked, well worked, thoroughly worked, soft, cottony, with its rustling cut off, with its crackling cut off.
mn21:19.1: "In the same way, *bhikkhus*, these are the five ways of speaking by which others, when speaking to you, might address you—
mn21:19.10: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you beneficially or unbeneficially;
mn21:19.11: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*] or with hate inside [*dosantarā*].
mn21:19.12: Even there, *bhikkhus*, you should train thus:
mn21:19.13: ‘Our mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and we will not utter evil speech. We will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*], with no hate inside [*na dosantarā*].
mn21:19.14: And we will dwell pervading that person with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettāsahagatena cetasā*], and with that as the basis we will dwell pervading the whole world with a mind like that cat-skin bag [*biḷārabhastāsamena cetasā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.’
mn21:19.15: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:19.6: with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*] or with hate inside [*dosantarā*].
mn21:19.7: When others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you in a timely way or in an untimely way;
mn21:19.8: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you truthfully or falsely;
mn21:19.9: when others speak to you, *bhikkhus*, they may address you gently or harshly;
mn21:2.1: Now at that time Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna was living overly closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*.
mn21:2.2: While Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna was living thus closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*—
mn21:2.3: if any *bhikkhu* spoke critically of those *bhikkhunis* in the presence of Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna, then Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna became angry and displeased, and even raised a dispute.
mn21:2.4: But if any *bhikkhu* spoke critically of Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna in the presence of those *bhikkhunis*, then those *bhikkhunis* became angry and displeased, and even raised a dispute.
mn21:2.5: That is how Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna was living closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*.
mn21:20.1: Even if bandits, *bhikkhus*, were to sever you limb from limb with a two-handled saw, whoever there gave rise to a corrupt mind would not be carrying out my instruction.
mn21:20.2: Even there, *bhikkhus*, you should train thus:
mn21:20.3: ‘Our mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and we will not utter evil speech. We will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with minds of goodwill [*mettacittā*], with no hate inside [*na dosantarā*].
mn21:20.4: And we will dwell pervading that person with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettāsahagatena cetasā*], and with that as the basis we will dwell pervading the whole world with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettāsahagatena cetasā*], abundant, expansive, immeasurable, free from hostility, free from ill will.’
mn21:20.5: That is how you should train, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:21.1: And you should frequently keep in mind this instruction through the simile of the saw, *bhikkhus*.
mn21:21.2: Do you see any kind of speech, slight or gross, that you would not endure?"
mn21:21.4: "Therefore, *bhikkhus*, frequently attend to this instruction through the simile of the saw.
mn21:21.5: That will be for your welfare and happiness for a long time."
mn21:21.7: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn21:21.8: The first, The Simile of the Saw, is finished.
mn21:3.1: Then a certain *bhikkhu* approached the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, that *bhikkhu* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn21:3.2: "Bhante, Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna is living overly closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*.
mn21:3.3: Living thus closely associated, bhante, Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna lives with the *bhikkhunis*—
mn21:3.4: if any *bhikkhu* speaks critically of those *bhikkhunis* in the presence of Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna, then Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna becomes angry and displeased, and even raises a dispute.
mn21:3.5: But if any *bhikkhu* speaks critically of Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna in the presence of those *bhikkhunis*, then those *bhikkhunis* become angry and displeased, and even raise a dispute.
mn21:3.6: That is how, bhante, Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna is living closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*."
mn21:4.2: "Come, *bhikkhu*, in my name tell the *bhikkhu* Moḷiyaphagguna:
mn21:4.3: ‘The Teacher, friend Phagguna, is calling you.’"
mn21:4.4: "Yes, bhante," that *bhikkhu* replied to the *Bhagavā*. Then he approached Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna, and having approached, he said this to him:
mn21:4.5: "The Teacher, friend Phagguna, is calling you."
mn21:4.6: "Yes, friend," Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna replied to that *bhikkhu*. Then he approached the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to Venerable Moḷiyaphagguna:
mn21:5.1: "Is it really true, Phagguna, that you are living overly closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*?
mn21:5.2: Is it really true, Phagguna, that while living thus closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*—
mn21:5.3: if any *bhikkhu* speaks critically of those *bhikkhunis* in your presence, then you become angry and displeased, and even raise a dispute?
mn21:5.4: But if any *bhikkhu* speaks critically of you in the presence of those *bhikkhunis*, then those *bhikkhunis* become angry and displeased, and even raise a dispute?
mn21:5.5: Is it really true, Phagguna, that you are living thus closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*?"
mn21:5.7: "Were you not, Phagguna, a son of a good family who went forth from the household life into homelessness out of faith?"
mn21:6.1: "That is not proper for you, Phagguna, as a son of a good family gone forth from the household life into homelessness out of faith: that you should live overly closely associated with the *bhikkhunis*.
mn21:6.10: Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone were to speak critically of you in your presence, then even there, Phagguna, you should abandon those desires tied to the household life and those thoughts [*vitakkā*] tied to the household life.
mn21:6.11: And there, Phagguna, you should train thus: ‘My mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and I will not utter evil speech. I will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with a mind of goodwill [*mettacitto*], with no hate inside [*na dosantaro*].’
mn21:6.12: That is how you should train, Phagguna.
mn21:6.13: Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone were to strike you with the hand, strike you with a clod, strike you with a stick, or strike you with a weapon, then even there, Phagguna, you should abandon those desires tied to the household life and those thoughts [*vitakkā*] tied to the household life.
mn21:6.14: And there, Phagguna, you should train thus: ‘My mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and I will not utter evil speech. I will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with a mind of goodwill [*mettacitto*], with no hate inside [*na dosantaro*].’
mn21:6.15: That is how you should train, Phagguna."
mn21:6.2: Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone in your presence were to speak critically of those *bhikkhunis*, then even there, Phagguna, you should abandon those desires tied to the household life and those thoughts [*vitakkā*] tied to the household life.
mn21:6.3: And there, Phagguna, you should train thus:
mn21:6.4: ‘My mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and I will not utter evil speech. I will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with a mind of goodwill [*mettacitto*], with no hate inside [*na dosantaro*].’
mn21:6.5: That is how you should train, Phagguna.
mn21:6.6: Therefore, Phagguna, even if someone in your presence were to strike those *bhikkhunis* with the hand, strike them with a clod, strike them with a stick, or strike them with a weapon, then even there, Phagguna, you should abandon those desires tied to the household life and those thoughts [*vitakkā*] tied to the household life.
mn21:6.7: And there, Phagguna, you should train thus:
mn21:6.8: ‘My mind [*cittaṁ*] will not be changed, and I will not utter evil speech. I will dwell compassionate for their welfare, with a mind of goodwill [*mettacitto*], with no hate inside [*na dosantaro*].’
mn21:6.9: That is how you should train, Phagguna.
mn21:7.10: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, on level ground at a great four-way crossroads there stood a thoroughbred chariot yoked, with the whip laid down.
mn21:7.11: A skilled trainer, a horse-taming charioteer, might mount it, take the reins in his left hand and the whip in his right, and drive it forward or back wherever and however he wished.
mn21:7.12: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, for those *bhikkhus* there was no instruction that needed to be given by me;
mn21:7.13: the only thing needed from me for those *bhikkhus* was the arousing of *sati* [*satuppādakaraṇīyaṁ*].
mn21:7.14: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, you too should abandon the unwholesome and apply yourselves to wholesome *dhammas*.
mn21:7.15: For in this way you too will attain growth, increase, and fullness in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*].
mn21:7.3: On that occasion, *bhikkhus*, I addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn21:7.4: ‘*Bhikkhus*, I eat one meal at one sitting.
mn21:7.5: While eating one meal at one sitting, *bhikkhus*, I recognize little illness, little affliction, lightness, strength, and comfortable dwelling.
mn21:7.6: Come, *bhikkhus*, you too eat one meal at one sitting.
mn21:7.7: While eating one meal at one sitting, *bhikkhus*, you too will recognize little illness, little affliction, lightness, strength, and comfortable dwelling.’
mn21:7.8: For those *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhus*, there was no instruction that needed to be given by me;
mn21:7.9: the only thing needed from me for those *bhikkhus* was the arousing of *sati* [*satuppādakaraṇīyaṁ*].
mn21:8.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, not far from a village or town there were a great grove of sāla trees.
mn21:8.2: And it was overgrown with castor shrubs.
mn21:8.3: Then some person might arise who wanted its benefit, wanted its welfare, wanted its security from bondage.
mn21:8.4: He would cut down those crooked sāla saplings that robbed the nourishment, carry them outside, and thoroughly clear out the interior of the grove.
mn21:8.5: But those sāla saplings that were straight and well-grown he would tend properly.
mn21:8.6: Then, after some time, that grove of sāla trees would attain growth, increase, and fullness.
mn21:8.7: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, you too should abandon the unwholesome and apply yourselves to wholesome *dhammas*.
mn21:8.8: For in this way you too will attain growth, increase, and fullness in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*].
mn21:9.1: Once upon a time, *bhikkhus*, in this very Sāvatthī there was a housewife named Vedehikā.
mn21:9.10: Suppose I were to test my mistress.’
mn21:9.11: Then, *bhikkhus*, Kāḷī the slave woman got up late.
mn21:9.12: Then, *bhikkhus*, Vedehikā the housewife said this to Kāḷī the slave woman:
mn21:9.15: ‘Why, girl, did you get up late?’
mn21:9.17: ‘Not for no reason, you wicked slave, that you got up late!’ Angry and displeased, she frowned.
mn21:9.18: Then, *bhikkhus*, it occurred to Kāḷī the slave woman:
mn21:9.19: ‘So my mistress really does have anger within, not that she does not have it.
mn21:9.2: And, *bhikkhus*, this good report had spread about Vedehikā the housewife:
mn21:9.20: It is just because I manage my work so well that my mistress, though she really has anger within, does not reveal it, and not because she does not have it.
mn21:9.21: Suppose I were to test my mistress even further.’
mn21:9.22: Then, *bhikkhus*, Kāḷī the slave woman got up even later.
mn21:9.23: Then, *bhikkhus*, Vedehikā the housewife said this to Kāḷī the slave woman:
mn21:9.26: ‘Why, girl, did you get up even later?’
mn21:9.28: ‘Not for no reason, you wicked slave, that you got up even later!’ Angry and displeased, she uttered angry and displeased words.
mn21:9.29: Then, *bhikkhus*, it occurred to Kāḷī the slave woman:
mn21:9.3: ‘Vedehikā the housewife is gentle; Vedehikā the housewife is mild; Vedehikā the housewife is calm.’
mn21:9.30: ‘So my mistress really does have anger within, not that she does not have it.
mn21:9.31: It is just because I manage my work so well that my mistress, though she really has anger within, does not reveal it, and not because she does not have it.
mn21:9.32: Suppose I were to test my mistress even further.’
mn21:9.33: Then, *bhikkhus*, Kāḷī the slave woman got up even later.
mn21:9.34: Then, *bhikkhus*, Vedehikā the housewife said this to Kāḷī the slave woman:
mn21:9.37: ‘Why, girl, did you get up late?’
mn21:9.39: ‘Not for no reason, you wicked slave, that you got up late!’ Angry and displeased, she took a door-pin and struck her on the head, splitting her head open.
mn21:9.4: Now Vedehikā the housewife, *bhikkhus*, had a slave woman named Kāḷī, who was skilled, not lazy, and managed her work well.
mn21:9.40: Then, *bhikkhus*, Kāḷī the slave woman, with her head split open and blood streaming, complained to the neighbors:
mn21:9.41: ‘See, ladies, the work of the gentle one!
mn21:9.42: See, ladies, the work of the mild one! See, ladies, the work of the calm one!
mn21:9.43: How could she, just because a single slave got up late, angry and displeased, take a door-pin and strike her on the head, splitting her head open?’
mn21:9.44: Then, *bhikkhus*, after some time this bad report spread about Vedehikā the housewife:
mn21:9.45: ‘Vedehikā the housewife is fierce; Vedehikā the housewife is not mild; Vedehikā the housewife is not calm.’
mn21:9.5: Then, *bhikkhus*, it occurred to Kāḷī the slave woman:
mn21:9.6: ‘This good report has spread about my mistress:
mn21:9.7: “Vedehikā the housewife is gentle; Vedehikā the housewife is mild; Vedehikā the housewife is calm.”
mn21:9.8: Now is it because my mistress really has anger within but does not reveal it, or because she does not have anger within?
mn21:9.9: Or is it just because I manage my work so well that my mistress, though she really has anger within, does not reveal it, and not because she does not have it?
